backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
The flexor retinaculum bridges the carpal bones, converting the carpal arch into the carpal tunnel. Attachments: scaphoid tubercle and trapezium laterally; pisiform and hook of hamate medially.

Clinical Pearl: Thickening compresses the median nerve, causing carpal tunnel syndrome — the most common entrapment neuropathy.

Memory Aid: "SHPT" — Scaphoid, Hamate hook, Pisiform, Trapezium.

The ulnar nerve and ulnar artery pass superficial to the retinaculum through Guyon's canal — they do NOT traverse the carpal tunnel.

Clinical Pearl: Cyclists may develop handlebar palsy (ulnar compression at Guyon's canal).

Memory Aid: "Ulnar goes OVER, Median goes UNDER."

The median nerve is the most superficial structure in the tunnel, sharing it with 9 tendons (4 FDS + 4 FDP + 1 FPL).

Clinical Pearl: CTS causes numbness in lateral 3.5 fingers and thenar wasting. Test with Phalen's test and Tinel's sign.

Memory Aid: "9 + 1 rule" — 9 tendons + 1 nerve pass through the tunnel.

Nine tendons: 4 FDS, 4 FDP, and 1 FPL. FDS tendons lie superficial in two rows, FDP tendons lie deep.

Clinical Pearl: Tenosynovitis increases tunnel pressure — a common cause of secondary CTS in rheumatoid arthritis.

Memory Aid: "4-4-1 in the tunnel."

The hook of hamate and pisiform are the medial attachments of the retinaculum.

Clinical Pearl: Fracture of the hook of hamate (racquet/bat sports) can injure the ulnar nerve in Guyon's canal.

Memory Aid: "Pisiform rides the FCU" — it's a sesamoid bone in the FCU tendon.
